core_claim: MiniPlasma is an LPE exploit targeting fully patched Windows systems. Released by Nightmare Eclipse as the sixth and final exploit in their June 2026 vulnerability wave. Works on current Patch Tuesday builds.

caption:
Nightmare Eclipse released MiniPlasma as the sixth and final exploit in their June 2026 wave. An LPE exploit targeting fully patched Windows systems with June 2026 Patch Tuesday installed.

The exploit chains two primitives that individually survived the patch: (1) A symbolic link race in a SYSTEM service that the patch did not fully close. (2) A token manipulation via a legitimate Windows API. Chained together = SYSTEM shell. Deterministic. No user interaction required.

Researcher demonstrates exploitation on a machine with all June 2026 updates applied. The patch closed the front door. The chain enters through the window. The researcher calls it 'patch residue' — mitigated primitives that combine in novel ways.

Patch Tuesday says 'protected.' Vulnerability scanners say 'clean.' But the chain uses two mitigated primitives in a novel combination. The whole is not the sum of the parts. No CVE means no scanner signature. No vendor advisory. Defenders assume patched = safe. The researcher proves patched = different attack surface.

Monitor for symbolic link creation in SYSTEM contexts. Audit token manipulation APIs (DuplicateTokenEx, ImpersonateLoggedOnUser). Hunt for patch-residue chains. Assume patched systems have new attack surfaces.

No patch for the combination. Microsoft may not assign CVE. Defenders must detect the behavior chain: symlink + token manipulation = SYSTEM. That pattern persists.

Patched does not mean immune. It means the attack surface has shifted.

Do you hunt for primitive chains on your patched endpoints?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[MiniPlasma, Nightmare Eclipse, Windows zero-day, LPE, fully patched, Patch Tuesday, patch residue, symbolic link race, token manipulation, behavioral detection, primitive chain]
comment_prompt: Do you hunt for primitive chains on your patched endpoints?
alt_text: Title card: MiniPlasma escapes the patch, fully patched yet still root. It is an LPE on current Patch Tuesday with no CVE, Nightmare Eclipse exploit six and the wave closer. A green plasma tendril slips through an Update shield checkmark to a SYSTEM prompt.

Nightmare Eclipse dropped MiniPlasma as the sixth and final exploit, targeting fully patched Windows 10 and 11 with June 2026 Patch Tuesday installed. No CVE assigned, chaining two post-patch primitives, six zero-days in six days. An Update history shows an exploit-verified badge.

MiniPlasma works on fully patched systems, chaining two primitives that survived June Patch Tuesday into a deterministic SYSTEM shell with no user interaction. The patch closed the front door, the chain enters the window. Two tendrils weave through a patch shield into a SYSTEM token.

The chain: a symbolic link race in a SYSTEM service that survives the patch gives arbitrary file write as SYSTEM, then a token manipulation via a legitimate Windows API converts it to a token. Both are mitigated alone but not together. Two chain links fuse into a SYSTEM crown.

Where defenders miss it: Patch Tuesday says protected and scanners say clean, but the chain combines two mitigated primitives, so the whole is not the sum of the parts and no CVE means no signature. A scanner report reads zero critical vulnerabilities under a red chain overlay.

Mitigation: monitor symbolic link creation in SYSTEM contexts, audit token APIs like DuplicateTokenEx and ImpersonateLoggedOnUser, hunt patch-residue chains, and assume patched systems have new attack surfaces. A split image of two separate mitigations versus the chained SYSTEM breach.
Patched does not mean immune. It means the attack surface has shifted. A Windows Update shield, half with a green checkmark and half with a green plasma chain slipping through to form a SYSTEM crown.
Closing question: Do you hunt for primitive chains on your patched endpoints? A behavioral detection rule for symlink and token chains sits on a clean dark end-card.

sources.source: Nightmare Eclipse blog: MiniPlasma Technical Details

narration.text: MiniPlasma is a local privilege escalation exploit that fully patched Windows 10 and 11 systems cannot stop.
Nightmare Eclipse released it as exploit six of six in a June 2026 wave, with no CVE assigned to the combination.
The exploit chains two primitives that each survived the patch: a symbolic link race in a SYSTEM service, and a token manipulation through a legitimate Windows API.
The first step lands arbitrary file write as SYSTEM; the second converts that write into a privileged token, producing a deterministic shell with no user interaction required.
Scanners return clean because the combination carries no CVE and no signature, leaving defenders who trust patch status alone with a genuine blind spot.
The defensive response is to monitor symbolic link creation in SYSTEM contexts, audit calls to DuplicateTokenEx and ImpersonateLoggedOnUser, and hunt for primitive chains rather than individual CVEs.
Patching closes the doors that Microsoft knows about, but this researcher showed that two separately mitigated primitives can open a new one.
If your team hunts primitive chains on patched endpoints, share your detection approach in the comments.

beats.caption: MiniPlasma escapes patch. Fully patched. Root.
Wave closer. June patch installed. No CVE. Patch residue.
Two primitives chain. Symlink race. Token API. SYSTEM.
Individually mitigated. Combined = exploit. Demo on updated.
Scanner clean. Patch protected. Chain enters window.
Monitor symlink. Audit token. Hunt chains. Shifted surface.
Patched not immune. Surface shifted. Pattern persists.
